response:
No, it is impossible to illuminate the entire plane with six light sources each emitting a $60^{\circ}$ sector. The total angular coverage provided by six such sectors is $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$. While this sum equals a full circle, covering the entire plane requires that every point in the infinite 2D space be illuminated. Light sources are located at specific points; any point in the plane that lies outside the union of the six $60^{\circ}$ sectors originating from these six sources will remain in darkness. Even if the sectors are arranged to cover all directions from a single central point, points far away from the sources or in the "gaps" between the source locations cannot be covered because the beams are directional and finite in their reach relative to the source positions. Furthermore, if the sources are distinct, the union of their sectors cannot cover the entire plane because the plane extends infinitely in all directions, and a finite number of directional sectors cannot cover every point simultaneously without leaving gaps or requiring the sources to be at every point.

P.S. The sum of angles is $360^{\circ}$ but geometric coverage of an infinite plane by finite directional sectors is not achievable.
